title: Which LLMs Get the Joke? Probing Non-STEM Reasoning Abilities with HumorBench
research_questions: Can LLMs explain sophisticated cartoon-caption humor by identifying the objective elements needed to understand the joke?
Does progress on STEM reasoning benchmarks transfer to non-STEM humor comprehension?
Do models trained exclusively on STEM reasoning data improve on HumorBench compared with their base models?
Does increasing test-time reasoning compute improve humor reasoning performance?
What types of humor elements remain difficult in the HumorBench Hard subset?

datasets_used.name: HumorBench
datasets_used.size: approximately 300 unique cartoon-caption pairs; 499 high-quality unique element annotations

datasets_used.name: New Yorker caption ranking (Humor in AI)

methods_proposed.name: HumorBench
methods_proposed.summary: A benchmark for evaluating LLM humor comprehension from textual cartoon descriptions and captions. Each cartoon-caption pair is annotated with 1–3 concise objective elements that a correct explanation should cover.

methods_proposed.name: Objective element annotation scheme
methods_proposed.summary: The paper separates objective joke-comprehension elements from subjective funniness or audience-preference factors. Elements are designed to be short, direct, verifiable, single-concept statements covering references, wordplay, implications, or similar mechanisms.

methods_proposed.name: GPT-4o autograder for element coverage
methods_proposed.summary: An LLM judge evaluates whether a model’s free-form joke explanation explicitly covers each annotated element. The autograder was validated against 300 human expert judgments and model outputs were truncated to the last 1000 tokens for length control.

methods_proposed.name: HumorBench Hard
methods_proposed.summary: A hard subset consisting of the 100 unique elements most often missed during benchmarking. The paper analyzes this subset by pass rates and by three humor categories: wordplay, cultural references, and toxic or shocking humor.

evaluation_methods.detail: For every cartoon-caption pair, the authors hand-annotated one to three objective elements; after iterative refinement, 499 high-quality unique element annotations remained.
GPT-4o was used as an autograder to decide whether each model explanation covered each anticipated answer point; in all evaluations, autograders, and benchmarks, “GPT-4o” refers to gpt-4o-2024-08-06.
The authors created a separate benchmark of 300 human expert judgments on explanations from GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Claude 3.7 Sonnet to validate the GPT-4o autograder.
A former chief cartoon editor of the New Yorker reviewed a random subset of 30 annotations and confirmed that all elements were fair and accurately captured the essential components of each joke.
Model performance is reported as accuracy or pass rate: an explanation is credited when the autograder judges that it covers the required objective element.
The paper reports Spearman’s rank correlations between HumorBench accuracy and GPQA Diamond, ARC-AGI, and LM Arena ELO.

headline_results.finding: The GPT-4o autograder achieved 92.00% overall accuracy against 300 human-labeled judgments, with 14.79% FPR and 6.51% FNR.
OpenAI o3 led the main HumorBench benchmarking at 87.5% accuracy.
DeepSeek R1 strongly outperformed DeepSeek V3 despite being based on the same 671B parameter architecture.
Claude 3.7 Sonnet with a thinking budget of 1024 tokens outperformed base Claude 3.7 Sonnet.
The correlation between HumorBench and ARC-AGI rose to ρ = 0.943 after removing o-series models.
On HumorBench-Hard, the best model was o3 at 59.85% accuracy, and no current LLM exceeded 60% accuracy.

key_findings: HumorBench evaluates humor comprehension rather than subjective funniness by grading free-form explanations against expert-annotated objective joke elements.

The GPT-4o autograder reached 92.00% overall accuracy on 300 human expert judgments but had a higher false positive rate than false negative rate, so the authors interpret HumorBench scores as upper bounds.
OpenAI o3 achieved the highest reported main HumorBench accuracy at 87.5%, while Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, and DeepSeek R1 were described as achieving approximately 80%.
HumorBench performance was positively correlated with GPQA Diamond, ARC-AGI, and LM Arena ELO, with the ARC-AGI correlation reaching ρ = 0.943 (p = 0.005) after excluding o-series models.

STEM-only reasoning-trained models such as DeepSeek R1 Zero and Phi-4 Reasoning Plus outperformed their base versions, which the paper interprets as evidence that abstract reasoning transfers to humor comprehension.

Test-time scaling produced mixed effects: increasing reasoning effort generally improved Qwen Plus and o-series models, while increasing Claude 3.7 Sonnet’s thinking budget beyond 1024 tokens hurt performance.
limitations: HumorBench uses textual descriptions of cartoons, which controls for vision problems but removes the challenge of recognizing visual cues.
The autograder is mildly lenient despite 92% agreement with humans, so reported scores should be seen as upper bounds.
Some element annotations may still contain errors or subjective interpretations despite refinement and validation.
The dataset size of 499 unique elements limits the statistical resolution of fine-grained analyses.
Cartoonstock images require licensing, and the paper positions a multimodal version that reintroduces original images as future work.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper analyzes a “toxic or shocking” humor category in the hard subset, reporting 25.7% representation in the entire set and 26.6% in the hard subset, but it does not present a broader safety or ethics discussion.

artifacts.other: The paper says it provides source links to original images; NYCC images are available through Hessel et al. (2023) and Jain et al. (2020), while Cartoonstock images require licensing.
